hints: Only the 13 'interesting' cards matter: the 2‚ô• plus 4 kings, 4 queens, 4 jacks.
Need exactly 3 cards before the 2‚ô•, one of each rank: 2‚ô• at position 4 of the 13 (prob 1/13).
Given 3 cards before, P(one K,Q,J)=C(4,1)¬≥/C(12,3)=64/220=16/55; total 16/715.
solution: Discard all cards except the $2\heartsuit$ and the $4$ kings, $4$ queens, $4$ jacks ($13$ cards). We need exactly one of each rank before the $2\heartsuit$, i.e. exactly $3$ cards before it (so the $2\heartsuit$ is $4$th of the $13$: probability $\tfrac1{13}$) and those three are one K, one Q, one J: $\dfrac{\binom41^3}{\binom{12}{3}}=\dfrac{64}{220}=\dfrac{16}{55}$. Thus $P=\tfrac1{13}\cdot\tfrac{16}{55}=\dfrac{16}{715}.$

hints: Each pick must leave the taxman at least one divisor; take primes early (while 1 is available) and order carefully.
Aim to give the taxman only {1,2,3,4,5} and keep {6,7,8,9,10}.
Order 7,9,6,10,8 works: taxman gets 1,3,2,5,4 ‚áí you score 40.
solution: Pick in the order $7,9,6,10,8$: the taxman takes $1$ (from $7$), $3$ (from $9$), $2$ (from $6$), $5$ (from $10$), $4$ (from $8$) ‚Äî each pick legal since a divisor is always still present. You keep $7+9+6+10+8=40$ while the taxman gets $1+2+3+4+5=15$. That's the maximum, so the answer is $40.$

hints: At the symmetric Nash both pick the same number of sides; test s=5.
With both rolling 1..5, payout E = (1/5)Œ£_x x¬∑P(Y‚â§7‚àíx).
solution: At the symmetric equilibrium both choose $5$ sides. With $X,Y$ uniform on $1$‚Äì$5$, your payout is $\mathbb{E}[X\mathbf 1\{X+Y\le7\}]=\tfrac15\sum_{x=1}^5 x\,P(Y\le7-x)=\tfrac15\big(1+2+\tfrac{12}5+\tfrac{12}5+2\big)=\tfrac15\cdot\tfrac{49}5=\tfrac{49}{25}$. With $s=5$, $sp=5\cdot\tfrac{49}{25}=\tfrac{49}{5}.$

hints: Given M,S: E[Y|M,S]=M and Var(Y|M,S)=S¬≤.
Law of total variance: Var(Y)=E[S¬≤]+Var(M).
E[S¬≤]=2 (Exp(1)), Var(M)=1 ‚áí Var(Y)=3.
solution: Conditionally, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid M,S]=M$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y\mid M,S)=S^2$. By the law of total variance, $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=\mathbb{E}[S^2]+\mathrm{Var}(M)$. For $\mathrm{Exp}(1)$, $\mathbb{E}[S^2]=2$ and $\mathrm{Var}(M)=1$, so $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=2+1=3.$

hints: Condition on the colour of the transferred cherry.
Transfer red (4/9): B has 7/10 red; transfer purple (5/9): B has 6/10 red.
solution: Condition on the transferred cherry. With probability $\tfrac49$ it's red, making B $7$ red of $10$; with probability $\tfrac59$ it's purple, making B $6$ of $10$. So $P(\text{red})=\tfrac49\cdot\tfrac7{10}+\tfrac59\cdot\tfrac6{10}=\tfrac{58}{90}=\tfrac{29}{45}.$

hints: This is the cover time of the complete graph K‚ÇÅ‚ÇÅ (each step moves to a uniform other node).
With k nodes seen, the chance the next step is new is (11‚àík)/10, so expected wait (10)/(11‚àík).
Sum these waits over k to get the expected number of steps.
Let the two break points be x,y ~ U(0,1); plot the unit square.
A triangle forms iff no piece exceeds 1/2; this carves out triangular regions.
Total favorable area = 1/4.
solution: Let the breaks be at $x,y\sim U(0,1)$. The three pieces form a triangle iff each is $<\tfrac12$ (no piece exceeds the sum of the others). Mapping the conditions onto the unit square gives two small triangles of total area $\tfrac14$, so the probability is $\tfrac14.$

hints: Each element falls in one of 4 equally likely categories: A only, B only, both, neither.
A‚äÜB forbids 'A only' for every element: probability 3/4 each.
solution: For each of the $5$ elements, its membership in $(A,B)$ is uniform over $\{$A only, B only, both, neither$\}$. $A\subseteq B$ requires no element to be 'A only', probability $\tfrac34$ each, so $P=(\tfrac34)^5=\tfrac{243}{1024}.$

hints: Let r=P(ever reach +1 from 0); by translation it's the same from any level.
For a downward-drifting walk (p<1/2), r=p/(1‚àíp).
solution: Let $r$ be the probability of ever moving up by $1$. Conditioning on the first step, $r=p+(1-p)r^2$ (a down step needs two net up-crossings). The relevant root for $p<\tfrac12$ is $r=\dfrac{p}{1-p}$. With $p=\tfrac13$, $r=\dfrac{1/3}{2/3}=\tfrac12.$

hints: Put‚Äìcall parity (no dividends, r‚âà0): C ‚àí P = S ‚àí K.
Arbitrage exists if the call trades under 12, not 14 ‚Äî so the threshold is 12.
solution: By put‚Äìcall parity $C-P=S-K$, so the fair call price is $C=P+(S-K)=2+(110-100)=12$. There's an arbitrage only if the call is below $12$ (your friend's $14$ is too high), so the answer is $12.$

hints: Count k√ók squares for each size k=1..8.
There are (9‚àík)¬≤ of size k, so total = Œ£_{k=1}^8 k¬≤ (reindexed).
solution: A $k\times k$ square can be placed in $(9-k)^2$ positions, so the total is $\sum_{k=1}^{8}(9-k)^2=\sum_{j=1}^{8}j^2=\dfrac{8\cdot9\cdot17}{6}=204.$

hints: Match the unordered pair of values; split on whether the first roll was a double.
First a double (prob 1/6): re-match prob 1/36. First distinct (5/6): re-match prob 2/36.
solution: Condition on the first roll. If it was a double (probability $\tfrac6{36}=\tfrac16$), the second matches with probability $\tfrac1{36}$; if it was two distinct values (probability $\tfrac56$), the second matches (either order) with probability $\tfrac2{36}$. So $P=\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac1{36}+\tfrac56\cdot\tfrac2{36}=\tfrac{1+10}{216}=\tfrac{11}{216}.$
